title: NSW + CPS Benchmark: Full Code
text:
This document contains the complete executable R script for nsw_cps_benchmark_lab.qmd.
# NSW + CPS benchmark lab: executable full analysis script.
# Run with:
#   Rscript docs/labs/nsw_cps_benchmark_lab_full_code.R

options(width = 120)

# ---- Step 1: Install and load required packages ----
required_packages <- c(
  "causaldata",
  "MatchIt",
  "WeightIt",
  "cobalt",
  "dplyr",
  "ggplot2"
)

missing_packages <- required_packages[!vapply(
  required_packages,
  requireNamespace,
  logical(1),
  quietly = TRUE
)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  message("Installing missing packages: ", paste(missing_packages, collapse = ", "))
  install.packages(missing_packages, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}

invisible(lapply(required_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))

# ---- Step 2: Load data and define benchmark vs observational samples ----
nsw <- causaldata::nsw_mixtape |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    treat = as.integer(treat),
    outcome = re78
  )

cps <- causaldata::cps_mixtape |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    treat = as.integer(treat),
    outcome = re78
  )

benchmark_dat <- nsw

obs_dat <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  nsw |>
    dplyr::filter(treat == 1L) |>
    dplyr::mutate(source = "NSW treated"),
  cps |>
    dplyr::filter(treat == 0L) |>
    dplyr::mutate(source = "CPS controls")
)

design_covariates <- c("age", "educ", "black", "hisp", "marr", "nodegree", "re74", "re75")

message("\n[Step 2] Observational data schema check")
obs_dat |>
  dplyr::select(source, treat, outcome, dplyr::all_of(design_covariates)) |>
  dplyr::glimpse()

# ---- Step 3: Experimental benchmark from NSW treated vs NSW controls ----
message("\n[Step 3] Experimental benchmark")
benchmark_summary <- benchmark_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    mean_re78 = mean(outcome, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re74 = mean(re74, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re75 = mean(re75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "NSW treated", "NSW controls")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, mean_re78, mean_re74, mean_re75)
print(benchmark_summary)

benchmark_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = benchmark_dat)
benchmark_att <- coef(summary(benchmark_fit))["treat", ]
print(benchmark_att)

# ---- Step 4: Raw observational comparison (NSW treated vs CPS controls) ----
message("\n[Step 4] Raw observational comparison")
raw_summary <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    mean_re78 = mean(outcome, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_educ = mean(educ, na.rm = TRUE),
    prop_black = mean(black == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re74 = mean(re74, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_re75 = mean(re75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "NSW treated", "CPS controls")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, mean_re78, mean_age, mean_educ, prop_black, mean_re74, mean_re75)
print(raw_summary)

raw_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = obs_dat)
raw_att <- coef(summary(raw_fit))["treat", ]
print(raw_att)

# ---- Step 5: Baseline balance and overlap diagnostics ----
message("\n[Step 5] Baseline balance diagnostics")
m_raw <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ age + educ + black + hisp + marr + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data = obs_dat,
  method = NULL,
  estimand = "ATT"
)

print(summary(m_raw, un = TRUE))
print(cobalt::bal.tab(m_raw, un = TRUE, binary = "std", m.threshold = 0.1))
print(cobalt::love.plot(
  m_raw,
  abs = TRUE,
  thresholds = c(m = 0.1),
  stars = "raw"
))

message("\n[Step 5] Propensity-score overlap summary")
ps_fit <- stats::glm(
  treat ~ age + educ + black + hisp + marr + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data = obs_dat,
  family = stats::binomial()
)

obs_dat <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::mutate(ps = predict(ps_fit, type = "response"))

overlap_bounds <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    min_ps = min(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_ps = max(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

common_support_low <- max(overlap_bounds$min_ps)
common_support_high <- min(overlap_bounds$max_ps)

overlap_summary <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = dplyr::n(),
    min_ps = min(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    p10_ps = as.numeric(stats::quantile(ps, 0.10, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p50_ps = as.numeric(stats::quantile(ps, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE)),
    p90_ps = as.numeric(stats::quantile(ps, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE)),
    max_ps = max(ps, na.rm = TRUE),
    in_common_support = mean(ps >= common_support_low & ps <= common_support_high),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "NSW treated", "CPS controls")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, n, min_ps, p10_ps, p50_ps, p90_ps, max_ps, in_common_support)
print(overlap_summary)

# ---- Step 6: Exact matching ----
message("\n[Step 6] Exact matching")
m_exact <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ black + hisp + marr + nodegree + educ,
  data = obs_dat,
  method = "exact",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

print(summary(m_exact, un = TRUE))

exact_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(m_exact)
exact_counts <- exact_dat |>
  dplyr::count(treat) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control"))
print(exact_counts)

print(cobalt::bal.tab(
  m_exact,
  data = obs_dat,
  un = TRUE,
  binary = "std",
  m.threshold = 0.1,
  addl = ~ age + re74 + re75
))

# ---- Step 7: Coarsened exact matching (CEM) ----
message("\n[Step 7] Coarsened exact matching (CEM)")
cem_cutpoints <- list(
  age = "q5",
  educ = 4,
  re74 = "q6",
  re75 = "q6"
)

m_cem <- MatchIt::matchit(
  treat ~ age + educ + black + hisp + marr + nodegree + re74 + re75,
  data = obs_dat,
  method = "cem",
  estimand = "ATT",
  cutpoints = cem_cutpoints
)

print(summary(m_cem, un = TRUE))
print(cobalt::bal.tab(m_cem, un = TRUE, binary = "std", m.threshold = 0.1))
print(cobalt::love.plot(
  m_cem,
  abs = TRUE,
  thresholds = c(m = 0.1),
  stars = "raw"
))

cem_dat <- MatchIt::match.data(m_cem)
cem_counts <- cem_dat |>
  dplyr::count(treat) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control"))
print(cem_counts)

# ---- Step 8: Entropy balancing and ESS diagnostics ----
message("\n[Step 8] Entropy balancing")
w_ebal <- WeightIt::weightit(
  treat ~ age + I(age^2) +
    educ + I(educ^2) +
    black + hisp + marr + nodegree +
    re74 + I(re74^2) +
    re75 + I(re75^2),
  data = obs_dat,
  method = "ebal",
  estimand = "ATT"
)

print(summary(w_ebal))
print(cobalt::bal.tab(w_ebal, un = TRUE, binary = "std", m.threshold = 0.1))
print(cobalt::love.plot(
  w_ebal,
  abs = TRUE,
  thresholds = c(m = 0.1),
  stars = "raw"
))

ess <- function(w) {
  (sum(w)^2) / sum(w^2)
}

obs_ebal <- obs_dat |>
  dplyr::mutate(w = w_ebal$weights)

ebal_weight_summary <- obs_ebal |>
  dplyr::group_by(treat) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    raw_n = dplyr::n(),
    ess = ess(w),
    max_weight = max(w),
    mean_weight = mean(w),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(group = dplyr::if_else(treat == 1L, "Treated", "Control")) |>
  dplyr::select(group, raw_n, ess, max_weight, mean_weight)
print(ebal_weight_summary)

# ---- Step 9: Compare all estimates against the experimental benchmark ----
message("\n[Step 9] Estimate comparison against benchmark")
exact_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = exact_dat, weights = weights)
cem_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = cem_dat, weights = weights)
ebal_fit <- stats::lm(outcome ~ treat, data = obs_ebal, weights = w)

get_treat_row <- function(fit, design_name) {
  out <- coef(summary(fit))
  data.frame(
    design = design_name,
    estimate = unname(out["treat", "Estimate"]),
    std_error = unname(out["treat", "Std. Error"]),
    t_value = unname(out["treat", "t value"]),
    p_value = unname(out["treat", "Pr(>|t|)"]),
    row.names = NULL,
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

experimental_estimate <- unname(benchmark_att["Estimate"])

estimate_comparison <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  get_treat_row(benchmark_fit, "Experimental benchmark (NSW RCT)"),
  get_treat_row(raw_fit, "Raw observational (NSW treated vs CPS)"),
  get_treat_row(exact_fit, "Exact matching"),
  get_treat_row(cem_fit, "CEM"),
  get_treat_row(ebal_fit, "Entropy balancing")
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    benchmark_gap = estimate - experimental_estimate,
    abs_benchmark_gap = abs(benchmark_gap)
  )

print(estimate_comparison)

message("\nDone: full NSW + CPS benchmark workflow executed.")

title: Lab: Titanic Teaching Example
text:
This lab turns the Titanic scaffold into a compact executed example for exact matching and propensity-score subclassification.






Note



This is a teaching example, not a literal policy-evaluation design. The value of causaldata::titanic is that the treatment groups, covariates, and support cells are easy to see on one page.

title: Lab: Titanic Teaching Example
text:
2 Design Choice
For a slide-friendly matching example, keep the setup deliberately simple:

Treatment: sex, coded here as female versus male
Outcome: survived
Pre-treatment covariates: ticket class and age
Teaching estimand: ATT for the female group

This choice keeps the design transparent. There are only two adjustment variables, both are discrete, and the implied exact-match cells are easy to inspect directly before using any propensity-score machinery.

title: Lab: Titanic Teaching Example

In this local installation, causaldata::titanic contains 2,201 passenger records with four variables: class, age, sex, and survived.

text:

There are 8 possible age-by-class cells. 7 are observed in the data, and all 7 observed cells contain both women and men. The only empty cell is Crew x Child.

6 Propensity-Score Subclassification
Subclassification is useful here as a bridge to higher-dimensional problems. With only two discrete covariates it does not buy much over exact matching, but it shows how a propensity-score design can recover almost the same adjusted comparison while relaxing the requirement that each design cell be used literally.

The key teaching result is that adjustment changes the estimate only modestly:

raw difference: 52.0 percentage points
exact matching: 50.0 percentage points

That is a useful contrast with higher-dimensional matching examples. Here, the adjustment task is so transparent that the class-by-age structure already explains most of what the design is doing.

title: Lab: Titanic Teaching Example
text:
8 Slide-Friendly Summary

Start with the cell structure. Titanic is useful because you can inspect the exact comparison cells directly.
Exact matching is feasible here because the covariates are low-dimensional and discrete.
Subclassification reaches nearly the same adjusted estimate, which makes it a clean bridge to settings where exact matching becomes too restrictive.
This is still a pedagogic example rather than a literal causal treatment effect, because sex is not a manipulable intervention and important determinants of survival are not observed.

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
Start with the causal question and estimand, not the method.
Judge balance together with overlap, retention, and weight concentration.
Exact matching, CEM, and entropy balancing solve different design problems.


Open with the report’s core claim: there is no default winner across methods. The audience should leave with a decision rule, not a favorite algorithm. Emphasize that the comparison is about design tradeoffs, not software output.

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
2 Why This Comparison Matters

These are design-stage tools for making treated and untreated units more comparable before outcome analysis.
They improve transparency more reliably than they guarantee validity.
A balanced design can still be weak if overlap is poor or key confounders are unobserved.


This slide aligns with the report’s early framing. Stress that matching and weighting are useful because they force explicit decisions about comparability. Also make the limit explicit: none of these methods solves unmeasured confounding.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
3 Report Workflow

Define the estimand and focal population.
Choose defensible pre-treatment covariates.
Diagnose raw imbalance and overlap.
Compare methods on the same target.
Estimate effects only after the design is credible.


Walk through this as the report’s organizing logic. If the audience remembers one process point, it should be that outcome modeling comes after design diagnostics, not before them.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
4 The Core Tradeoff
You usually cannot maximize all three at once:

transparent comparison rules
strong observed balance
preservation of the original treated population

That tradeoff is the report’s main theme.

This is the design triangle from the report in plain language. Set up the rest of the deck by saying each method sits in a different part of this tradeoff space.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
5 Main Worked Example
The main article uses MatchIt::lalonde to estimate an ATT.

Focal group: programme participants
Pre-treatment covariates include age, education, race, marital status, degree status, and prior earnings
Teaching goal: compare balance gains against information loss

Main report

Keep this brief. The purpose is not to reteach the whole dataset, but to remind the audience that the report’s substantive question is the earnings effect for the treated group.

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
6 What The Main Report Finds








Method
Main strength
Main cost




Exact matching
Most auditable design
Residual imbalance on prior earnings


CEM
Clearest matched-sample compromise
Treated sample shrinks from 185 to 81


Entropy balancing
Preserves all treated units

Present this as the report’s headline comparison. Use the numbers to make the cost concrete. Do not oversell any one method: exact matching is transparent, CEM is the strongest matched-sample compromise, and entropy balancing is strongest when preserving the treated population is the priority.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
7 Main Report Decision Rule

Use exact matching when the design is genuinely low-dimensional and auditability matters most.
Use CEM when you want an inspectable matched sample across mixed covariate types.
Use entropy balancing when retaining the original treated population matters most and the weights stay credible.


This slide translates the report into evaluator guidance. Say explicitly that method choice follows the question and overlap conditions, not analyst preference.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
8 Why Add A Second Dataset
The Black Politicians lab tests whether the same logic holds outside the lalonde example.

Data: causaldata::black_politicians
Training comparison: leg_black
Outcome: responded
Shared target: ATT

Lab page

Frame this as a stress test of the report’s narrative. The point is not that leg_black is a policy treatment; the point is to teach comparability, overlap, and estimand drift on a second dataset.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
9 Black Politicians: Design Problem

Raw response rate is 0.393 for Black legislators versus 0.425 for non-Black legislators.
Mean district Black share is 0.517 versus 0.063.
Democratic share is 0.978 versus 0.501.
Raw max absolute SMD across the audited covariates is 2.247.

This is a structural comparability problem, not a small baseline mismatch.

Use this slide to slow down and show why the design burden is real. The audience should see that raw group differences are substantial before any method is applied.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
10 Black Politicians: Exact Matching

Best used here as a feasibility check, not the preferred final design.
It retains all 364 treated units on the chosen discrete covariates.
It still leaves open the question of continuous comparability on district composition and resources.


Connect this back to the report’s exact matching discussion. Retention alone is not evidence of a good design. Explain that exact agreement on a few discrete variables can still miss large continuous differences.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
11 Black Politicians: CEM

CEM keeps the matching logic visible while handling continuous covariates through bins.
Max adjusted absolute SMD falls to 0.093.
The treated sample falls from 364 to 300.

This is the lab’s clearest matched-sample compromise.

Make the parallel to the main report explicit: CEM again plays the role of the most legible matched-sample design. Note that the cost is visible sample pruning, which shifts the practical target toward treated units with support.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
12 Black Politicians: Entropy Balancing

Included adjusted mean differences are driven to 0.000.
The control ESS falls to about 74.8.
The maximum control weight rises to about 209.

The balance table improves, but the weighted comparison becomes fragile.

This is the same caution as in the report, but sharper. Tell the audience that entropy balancing solves the observed mean-balance problem while moving the design cost into a much more concentrated weighted comparison.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
13 What Changes Across Designs

The simple treat estimate is not stable across raw, exact, CEM, and entropy-balanced designs.
That instability is the point of the exercise, not a nuisance.
Design diagnostics need to be interpreted before any substantive effect claim.

Evaluated lab output

Use this slide to explain why the report separates design from effect interpretation. If the estimate moves materially when the comparison group changes, the design choice is doing real work and must be defended.
crumbs: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference

title: Matching and Weighting for Causal Inference
text:
14 Practical Evaluator Takeaway

Matching and weighting are useful when rich pre-treatment covariates are available and stronger quasi-experimental designs are not.
Better balance is not enough if the design no longer represents the intended ATT.
The right question is not “which method wins?” but “which cost is acceptable for this causal question?”


Bring the deck back to the report’s evaluator framing. Mention that matching should sit inside a wider quasi-experimental toolkit rather than act as the default response to observational data.

title: Matching and Weighting Learning Resources
text:
This website is a training and reference space for design-stage causal inference using matching and weighting methods in observational evaluation.
Its core goal is to show how to design, diagnose, and estimate observational comparisons responsibly using:

exact matching
coarsened exact matching
entropy balancing for weighting

The material is organized around a practical evaluator workflow rather than a software-first workflow. The emphasis throughout the site is that:

matching and weighting are design tools, not substitutes for causal assumptions
balance must be assessed together with retained sample size and overlap
entropy balancing should be treated as a weighting method, not as a matching method

The intended audience is evaluators, analysts, and staff who need a practical introduction to quasi-experimental design choices in programme and policy evaluation. The framing is especially aligned with training and capacity-building use cases, where methods need to be technically sound, explainable, and auditable.

title: Matching and Weighting Learning Resources
text:
2 How The Site Is Structured
The site is split into three main areas:

Reports: longer methods write-ups and their supporting audit materials
Additional Labs: worked examples that extend the core material to other datasets and teaching scenarios
Slides: presentation decks that support training delivery and lab handoff

If you are new to the site, the recommended sequence is:

Start with the main report.
Use the full-code and QA pages when you want implementation detail or audit traceability.
Move into the labs for additional worked examples.
Use the slide decks when you want a delivery-ready teaching version.
